retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Intrusive Luxation Injuries
retrieved_docs.content: The tooth should be allowed to reposition spontaneously, irrespective of the direction of the displacement. Spontaneous improvement in the position of the intruded tooth usually occurs within six months but can take up to one year in some cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- History and Physical -- Intrusive Luxation
retrieved_docs.content: Intrusive luxation is the apical displacement of the tooth, usually through the labial bone plate. The tooth may present as almost or complete disappearance of the tooth into the socket. The tooth can be palpated labially in clinical examination. Radiographic examination is recommended for diagnostic purposes and to determine the orientation of the intrusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- History and Physical -- Extrusive Luxation
retrieved_docs.content: The partial displacement of the tooth out of its socket is known as extrusive luxation. The tooth appears elongated and has increased mobility. Occlusal interference may be present. A radiographic examination is recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Intrusive Luxation Injuries
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical examination is recommended after one week, six to eight weeks, six months, and one year. Further follow-up when the child reaches the age of six is recommended for a severe intrusion to monitor the eruption of the permanent successor. Radiographic examination is recommended only if there are clinical signs of pathosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Extrusive Luxation Injuries
retrieved_docs.content: A clinical follow-up examination is recommended after one week and six to eight weeks. If an unfavorable outcome is likely, yearly clinical follow-up is recommended until the permanent tooth erupts. Radiographic examination is recommended only if there are clinical signs of pathosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- History and Physical -- Lateral Luxation
retrieved_docs.content: The displacement of the tooth in a palatal/lingual or labial direction is known as lateral luxation. The tooth will be immobile and occlusal interference may be present. A radiographic examination is recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Extrusive Luxation Injuries
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment decisions are based on the degree of displacement, mobility, occlusal interferences, root formation, and the child's cooperation. If the tooth is not interfering with the occlusion, leave it to reposition spontaneously. If the tooth is excessively mobile or extruded >3 mm, extract it under local anesthesia.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Lateral Luxation Injuries
retrieved_docs.content: If there is minimal or no occlusal interference, the tooth should be allowed to reposition spontaneously, usually within six months. If the tooth is severely displaced and there is a risk of ingestion or aspiration, the tooth should be extracted under local anesthesia.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Lateral Luxation Injuries
retrieved_docs.content: Alternatively, for severe displacement, gently reposition the tooth, and if unstable in its new position, apply a flexible splint for four weeks attached to the adjacent uninjured teeth. Clinical examination is recommended after one week (four weeks for splint removal if used), six to eight weeks, six months, and one year. If an unfavorable outcome is likely, yearly clinical follow-up is recommended until the permanent tooth erupts. Radiographic examination is recommended only if there are clinical signs of pathosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- History and Physical -- Tooth Luxations
retrieved_docs.content: As it takes a significant amount of force to cause an extrusive, intrusive, or lateral tooth luxation, a thorough examination of the oral cavity should be performed to evaluate for other traumatic injuries, including lacerations, gingival contusions, and mandibular or midface fractures. Radiographs may be useful for assessing the exact positioning of the tooth and also aid in identifying the extent of the injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- History and Physical -- Intrusive Luxation
retrieved_docs.content: When the tooth's apex is displaced toward or through the labial bone plate (away from the permanent tooth germ), the apical tip can be seen, and the image of the tooth will appear shorter (foreshortened) than the contralateral tooth. When the tooth's apex is displaced toward the permanent tooth germ, the apical tip cannot be visualized, and the image of the tooth will appear elongated.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- History and Physical -- Subluxation
retrieved_docs.content: The tooth will show tenderness and increased mobility but no displacement. Bleeding from the gingival crevice may be noted. Radiographs should be taken for diagnostic purposes and to establish a baseline.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: If the coronal fragment is not displaced, no treatment is required. If the coronal fragment is displaced and is not excessively mobile, leave the coronal fragment to reposition spontaneously, even if occlusal interference is present. A clinical follow-up examination is recommended after one week, six to eight weeks, and one year. Continue clinical follow-up yearly until the eruption of the permanent successor.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- History and Physical -- Tooth Luxations
retrieved_docs.content: In dental trauma, a physical exam demonstrating a tooth with abnormal loosening is consistent with tooth subluxation. If a tooth is found to be displaced out of its alveolar socket in an incisal/axial direction, it is considered to be an extrusive luxation. This is in contrast to an intrusive luxation, which involves the displacement of the tooth apically into the alveolar bone. Lateral luxations consist of a tooth displaced in any lateral direction and are commonly associated with alveolar socket wall fractures. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: If the coronal fragment is displaced, excessively mobile, and interfering with occlusion, there are two options: a) extract the loose coronal fragment only, leaving the apical fragment in place to be reabsorbed; or b) gently reposition the loose coronal fragment. If the fragment is unstable in its new position, stabilize it with a flexible splint attached to adjacent uninjured teeth for four weeks.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Subluxation Injuries
retrieved_docs.content: No treatment is required. A clinical follow-up examination is recommended after one week and six to eight weeks. If an unfavorable outcome is likely, yearly clinical follow-up is recommended until the permanent tooth erupts. Radiographic examination is only required if there are clinical signs of pathosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2107
retrieved_docs.content: Skull and face fractures, described in Arabic as كسر في قبو القحف, refer to injuries involving breaks in the cranial vault and the facial bones, encompassing a range of traumatic damage to the head and facial structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: diverse injury types (intracranial, skeletal, facial), their specific anatomical subcategories, corresponding treatment modalities, and long‑term sequelae are interrelated, while also incorporating psychosocial determinants such as aggression that may influence injury occurrence and outcome. This integrated framework supports comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with complex traumatic or toxic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 797
retrieved_docs.content: particular bones) are nested under a universal category of late effects, which also encompasses non‑fracture injuries such as burns and frostbite. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in systematically assessing and managing the long‑term sequelae of diverse traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injuries or internal injuries are associated with open wounds (جرح مفتوح), indicating that such traumatic forces often result in visible tissue disruption.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1781
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injury or internal injury is associated with the medical condition referred to as إصابة (injury), indicating that such traumatic forces commonly result in injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 227
retrieved_docs.content: kosti” (facial bones) to the therapeutic context “treatment; facial fracture or dislocation,” specifying that interventions targeting the facial bones are directed toward managing facial fractures or dislocations. Collectively, these interrelated hierarchies integrate psychological index terms, injury classifications, anatomical sites, specific traumatic diagnoses, fracture subtypes, superficial injuries, late sequelae, violent behaviors, and diagnostic‑therapeutic pathways, providing a comprehensive framework for documentation, assessment, and patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: and skeletal fractures, their classification as wounds, the circumstances that generate them (assault, self‑harm), and a comprehensive hierarchy of late‑effect outcomes—including functional sequelae, behavioral changes such as aggression, and psychosocial conditions—providing clinicians with an integrated framework for injury documentation, treatment planning, and long‑term patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: sequelae that are fracture‑specific—late effects of lower‑limb fracture, upper‑limb fracture, and femoral‑neck fracture—alongside other late consequences such as burns, etching and freezing, unspecified upper‑extremity injury, and injury of multiple body regions. Both الآثار المتأخرة لإصابة غير محددة and superficial injury; contusion share the behavioral group العداء/العدوان (aggression/hostility), which further contains السادية المازوخية (sadomasochism), illustrating a psychosocial dimension linked to injury outcomes. Procedurally, the relation direct_procedure_site_of connects facial bones (obličejové kosti) with treatment; facial fracture or dislocation, indicating that interventions for facial fractures or dislocations are performed at the facial bone sites. Collectively, these relationships form a structured network in which fractures—whether of multiple bones, the spine, trunk, limbs, femoral neck, or skull vault—are intricately linked to specific late‑effect conditions,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2364
retrieved_docs.content: Asphyxiation and strangulation are medical events that are classified under the category of other injuries and conditions due to external causes, indicating that they arise from external mechanisms rather than internal disease processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of anterior fossa and fracture of middle fossa are both classified as إِصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury). Each represents a specific type of skull‑base fracture, with the anterior fossa fracture involving the front portion of the cranial base and the middle fossa fracture affecting the central region of the cranial base. Both conditions fall under the broader category of injuries inside the skull, highlighting their shared nature as intracranial traumatic lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2430
retrieved_docs.content: Other miscellaneous procedures are classified as a subset of other nonoperative procedures, indicating that they fall under the broader category of nonoperative interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 199
retrieved_docs.content: network delineates hierarchical relationships among poisoning, various injury types (intracranial, skeletal, superficial), their anatomical localizations, associated therapeutic interventions (e.g., treatment of facial fracture or dislocation), and both physical and behavioral long‑term outcomes, providing a comprehensive structure for patient assessment, management, and follow‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: grouped under a unified late‑effect classification that is further subdivided by anatomical region. This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 35
retrieved_docs.content: or dislocation” (direct_morphology_of كسر). The Dutch concept Late gevolgen van letsel van multipele lichaamsregio's (late consequences of injury of multiple body regions) is mapped from “superficial injury; contusion,” reinforcing the view that contusions are considered a type of late effect of an unspecified injury. Collectively, these relationships integrate injury and fracture terminology across Arabic, Dutch, Czech, and English, delineate hierarchical classifications of acute trauma and their chronic sequelae, specify anatomical sites of injury and treatment, and provide a structured basis for clinical assessment, coding, and management of traumatic injuries.
